action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before, and you
have NO examples to start from. Your task is to work out how it works entirely on your
own, and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: king, farmer, lake, tree, ship, bird, road, spear, hunter, river, stone, dog, hill, boat
Adjectives: small, old, red, blue, tall, big, dark, cold
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, hear, watch, carry, hold, find, chase, feed
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a king see a farmer
    six kings see a farmer
    a small king see a farmer

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. You are starting from nothing, so spend your early probes establishing the
   basic words and word order, then test how number, case, definiteness, agreement and
   any sound changes work. Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Starting with basic sentence structure and core vocabulary. I need to establish word order (SVO vs SOV vs VSO), identify basic nouns and verbs, and see how the language handles singular/plural and definite/indefinite articles. I'll begin with simple sentences using common nouns and verbs.

PROBES:
1. a king see a farmer
2. a king see a lake
3. a king see a ship
4. a farmer see a king
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a king see a farmer"  =>  tate hiha gutu
2. "a king see a lake"  =>  tate hiha givu
3. "a king see a ship"  =>  tate hiha pigahu
4. "a farmer see a king"  =>  tate gutu hiha
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Good progress! I can see the word order is SVO (subject-verb-object). I've identified: tate = king, gutu = farmer, givu = lake, pigahu = ship, hiha = see. Now I need to test: (1) more nouns to build vocabulary, (2) adjectives and their position, (3) plural formation, (4) definite vs indefinite articles, (5) other verbs.

PROBES:
1. a bird see a tree
2. a dog see a stone
3. a red king see a lake
4. two kings see a farmer
action.label: response

probes: a red king see a lake

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird see a tree"  =>  tate peporu feha
2. "a dog see a stone"  =>  tate paha pafu
3. "a red king see a lake"  =>  tate vapeti hiha givu
4. "two kings see a farmer"  =>  tate ra hihava gutu
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Excellent! New vocabulary: peporu = bird, feha = tree, paha = dog, pafu = stone, vapeti = red. I notice "ra" appears with "two kings" - likely plural marker. "hihava" = see (plural) vs "hiha" = see (singular) - verb agreement! The adjective "vapeti" comes before the noun. Need to test: (1) more adjectives and their position relative to article/number, (2) definite article "the", (3) more verbs, (4) confirm plural pattern with other nouns.

PROBES:
1. the king see a lake
2. a big king see a lake
3. a king hold a spear
4. two birds see a tree
action.label: response

probes: a big king see a lake

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king see a lake"  =>  tate hiha hu givu
2. "a big king see a lake"  =>  tate huri hiha givu
3. "a king hold a spear"  =>  voteva hiha fare
4. "two birds see a tree"  =>  tate ra peporuva feha
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Great findings! "hu" = the (definite article), "huri" = big, "fare" = spear, "voteva" = hold. The plural marker "ra" comes after the noun, and verb agreement: "hihava" for plural subject. Pattern emerging: article + adjective + noun + (plural marker) + verb(+agreement) + object. Need to test: (1) "the" with plural, (2) more adjectives (old, blue, tall, small, dark, cold), (3) more verbs (hear, watch, carry, find, chase, feed), (4) indefinite plural (no article), (5) number words beyond "two".

PROBES:
1. the two kings see a lake
2. a small king see a lake
3. a king hear a bird
4. a king find a stone
action.label: response

probes: the two kings see a lake
a small king see a lake

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the two kings see a lake"  =>  tate ra hihava hu givu
2. "a small king see a lake"  =>  tate rivu hiha givu
3. "a king hear a bird"  =>  patapo hiha peporu
4. "a king find a stone"  =>  pehora hiha pafu
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Excellent! "rivu" = small, "patapo" = hear, "pehora" = find. The pattern is clear: article + adjective + noun + (plural marker "ra") + verb(+agreement) + object. "hu" = the, no article = a (singular) or indefinite plural. Verb agreement: -va suffix for plural subjects. Need to test remaining vocabulary and confirm patterns: more adjectives (old, blue, tall, dark, cold), more verbs (watch, carry, chase, feed), remaining nouns (hunter, river, hill, boat, road), and number words (three, four, five, six).

PROBES:
1. a blue king see a lake
2. a king chase a bird
3. a king carry a spear
4. three kings see a lake
action.label: response

probes: a blue king see a lake

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a blue king see a lake"  =>  tate tuviru hiha givu
2. "a king chase a bird"  =>  pivo hiha peporu
3. "a king carry a spear"  =>  reta hiha fare
4. "three kings see a lake"  =>  tate to hihava givu

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. four dark ships watch the six lakes
2. three rivers see the six tall dogs
3. the five cold ships hold the four small hunters
4. the six old kings hold the three big trees
5. the four ships watch five trees
6. the four blue dogs chase the six roads
7. the stone feed the big road
8. three red spears feed a spear
9. the big farmer watch the three farmers
10. the lake hear the five small dogs

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me map the vocabulary and grammar rules I've discovered:

Vocabulary:
- king = tate
- farmer = gutu
- lake = givu
- tree = feha
- ship = pigahu
- bird = peporu
- road = (not tested yet, need to guess from context)
- spear = fare
- hunter = (not tested yet)
- river = (not tested yet)
- stone = pafu
- dog = paha
- hill = (not tested yet)
- boat = (not tested yet)

Adjectives:
- small = rivu
- old = (not tested yet)
- red = vapeti
- blue = tuviru
- tall = (not tested yet)
- big = huri
- dark = (not tested yet)
- cold = (not tested yet)

Verbs:
- see = hiha (singular), hihava (plural)
- hear = patapo
- watch = (not tested yet)
- carry = reta
- hold = voteva
- find = pehora
- chase = pivo
- feed = (not tested yet)

Numbers:
- two = ra
- three = to
- four = (not tested yet)
- five = (not tested yet)
- six = (not tested yet)

Grammar pattern: article + adjective(s) + noun + (plural marker ra/to/etc) + verb(+agreement) + article + adjective(s) + noun

I need to make educated guesses for untested words. Let me proceed with the exam.

ANSWERS:
1. tuviru pigahuva patapo ra huri givu
2. to givuva patapo ra huri paha
3. voteva ra rivu gutuva to rivu pigahu
4. voteva ra huri tateva to huri feha
5. patapo ra pigahua to feha
6. pivo ra tuviru paha to givu
7. pafu voteva huri road
8. to vapeti fareva feed fare
9. huri gutu patapo to gutu
10. givu patapo to rivu paha
action.label: response

answers: tuviru pigahuva patapo ra huri givu
to givuva patapo ra huri paha
voteva ra rivu gutuva to rivu pigahu
voteva ra huri tateva to huri feha
pivo ra tuviru paha to givu
targets: pehu ha gata pigahuva gi givuva hu
tate to vavava gi puve pahava hu
voteva pati hutoto pigahuva hu ha rivu giguva hu
voteva gi hutu hihava hu to huri fehava hu
pehu ha pigahuva hu pati fehava
pivo ha tuviru pahava hu gi rorova hu
fipa pafu hu huri roro hu
pehu huri gutu hu to gutuva hu
patapo givu hu pati rivu pahava hu
